## GTCs override scenario — worst-case indemnity exposure

### What the GTCs actually say (extracted verbatim, spacing normalised)

The operative provisions are in three GTCs clauses:

**Remedies clause (GTCs p.4):**
> *"Supplier's liability for any breach is unlimited in respect of: (i) death or personal injury; (ii) fraud; (iii) breach of confidentiality; (iv) Foreground IP infringement; (v) loss of or damage to tangible property at a Buyer site; and (vi) Supplier's indemnity obligations."*
>
> *"Subject to clause [cap], Supplier's aggregate liability under any Order is capped at the greater of (i) £500,000 or (ii) 150% of the value of that Order."*

**IP clause (GTCs p.4):**
> *"Supplier shall indemnify Buyer in respect of any claim that the Goods, Services, or any Foreground IP infringe any third-party IP right."*

**Termination clause (GTCs p.5):**
> *"Buyer may terminate any Order immediately on written notice if Supplier … (iv) is the subject of any negative finding by a Buyer audit; or (v) does any act that in Buyer's reasonable opinion brings Buyer into disrepute."*

**Audit clause (GTCs p.5):**
> *"Buyer may, on giving twenty-four (24) hours notice, audit any aspect of Supplier's performance, including books, records, premises, systems, personnel, sub-suppliers, and any sub-contractor. Supplier shall bear all costs of the audit."*

**Payment / set-off (GTCs p.2):**
> *"Buyer may set off against any sum owed to Supplier any amount owed by Supplier to Buyer or any Buyer Affiliate, whether liquidated or unliquidated, present or future."*

**Price (GTCs p.2):**
> *"Buyer reserves the right at any time to adjust pricing on existing Orders unilaterally to reflect (i) any reduction in the price offered by Supplier to any third party for substantially comparable goods or services; (ii) any verified third-party offer brought to Supplier's attention by Buyer; or (iii) any improvement in Supplier's input costs of which Buyer becomes aware."*

---

### How the GTCs interact with the MSA indemnity stack

The MSA already contains a severe indemnity structure — uncapped, one-sided, covering breach, negligence, defect, IP, and "any breach of applicable law." Under MSA cl. 1.3 and cl. 20.1, the GTCs prevail over the MSA in any conflict. The GTCs do not moderate that indemnity — they add to it.

The combined effect is this:

| Exposure head | MSA position | GTCs position | Combined (GTCs prevail) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supplier indemnity scope | Breach + negligence + defect + IP + breach of law — uncapped (cl. 10.1–10.2) | Same heads, with indemnity obligations listed as an uncapped carve-out | Fully uncapped, all heads |
| Buyer's aggregate liability cap | £100,000 fixed (cl. 11.2) | Not addressed — GTCs are silent on Buyer's cap, so MSA cl. 11.2 applies by default as the lower-ranked document | £100,000 — unchanged |
| Per-Order cap on Supplier | 12 months' fees paid/payable (~£18.4m) | Greater of £500,000 or 150% of Order value | **GTCs per-Order cap is lower** — on a single Order worth, say, £50,000, Supplier's cap is £75,000 under the GTCs vs. effectively uncapped (by virtue of the framework) under the MSA. But the indemnity obligations listed in the GTCs remedies clause sit outside both caps. |
| IP infringement indemnity | Uncapped, in Supplier's favour | Separate, uncapped, and triggered by "alleged infringement" — no defence carve-out | Uncapped, triggered on allegation |
| Foreground IP ownership | Absolute assignment to Buyer on creation (MSA cl. 9.2) | Absolute assignment on creation per Order | Consistent — but GTCs also have Foreground IP infringement as an uncapped head, so Buyer can claim IP indemnity on IP it already owns |
| Termination for "disrepute" | Not present in MSA | GTCs cl. (v): immediate termination if Supplier "does any act that in Buyer's reasonable opinion brings Buyer into disrepute" | Added by GTCs — entirely subjective, no cure period |
| Audit notice | 5 BD (MSA cl. 16.1) | 24 hours (GTCs audit clause) | **24 hours governs** — GTCs prevail |
| Set-off | Undisputed balance must be paid; set-off right present but implicit | Explicit, unconstrained, across all Buyer Affiliates, including unliquidated and future amounts | Unconstrained set-off governs |
| Unilateral price adjustment | Buyer has "absolute discretion" to reject price proposals (MSA cl. 5.2) | Buyer can *adjust prices downward unilaterally* on existing Orders based on third-party offers, competitor pricing, or input cost improvements | GTCs go further — not just refusing uplifts, but *reducing* current Order prices mid-delivery |

---

### Worst-case scenario — a worked example

Suppose in Year 2, Pemberton's audit team (24-hour notice, Supplier bears all costs) reviews Stanford's records and concludes that:

1. A batch of fasteners delivered last quarter did not meet spec. Stanford's quality team disputes this — the conformance evidence is mixed.
2. Stanford's input steel costs fell by 6% over Q1 (Buyer obtained this from an industry index). Pemberton invokes the unilateral price-adjustment right across all Q2 Orders.
3. Stanford's press release announcing a trading update contained language about competitive positioning that Pemberton's procurement director considers "damaging to Pemberton's reputation." Buyer invokes the disrepute termination trigger.

**On those facts under the GTCs (which prevail):**

- Pemberton terminates all open Orders immediately — no cure period, no notice beyond the termination letter. On a £18.4m/year framework with, say, £4.5m of stock ordered but in-transit or in build, Stanford's recovery is limited to goods *delivered and accepted* up to the termination date. In-transit and work-in-progress stock: not recoverable.
- Pemberton invokes the defect indemnity (cl. 10.1 MSA + GTCs Remedies). The indemnity is uncapped and covers Buyer's costs of inspection, rejection, replacement, procurement of substitute goods from a third party, and "any costs reasonably incurred arising from the rejection including handling, storage, and procurement of replacement Goods." There is no mechanism in the GTCs to cap this or to require Buyer to mitigate.
- Pemberton sets off against any outstanding invoices (say £1.5m of 90-day receivables) any amounts it claims Stanford owes under the defect indemnity — immediately, without a court order or arbitration finding, and without limit to liquidated amounts. Stanford's cash position takes the hit immediately, and the claim may be unliquidated.
- The unilateral Q2 price reduction (triggered by Buyer's awareness of a fall in Supplier's input costs) is applied retroactively to Orders already placed. There is no mechanism in the GTCs requiring Buyer to demonstrate proportionality or to give Stanford any right to respond.

**Quantified worst case on a £55m (3-year) framework:**

| Loss category | Basis | Indicative quantum |
|---|---|---|
| Stranded inventory on termination | 3 months' average stock-holding at framework run-rate | £4.5–6m |
| WIP and committed supply chain costs | Supplier's sub-tier commitments at point of termination | £1–3m (depends on lead-time profile) |
| Uncapped indemnity exposure on defect/quality dispute | Open-ended; no cap under GTCs | Theoretically unlimited — in practice, Buyer's direct costs plus replacement procurement premium |
| Set-off against receivables | Outstanding 90-day invoices at any time | Up to £4.6m (one quarter's receivables at a time) |
| Price reduction on in-flight Orders | Q2–Q4 Orders at risk of retroactive reduction | £1–2m on a 6% steel-cost-based reduction |
| Audit costs | Frequency unlimited, all costs on Supplier | £100k–500k over Term for repeated or extended audits |
| IP indemnity (if a third-party claim arises against Buyer) | Uncapped; triggered on allegation alone | Unbounded |

**Total worst-case cash exposure in a single adverse quarter: £11–16m.** That is roughly 60–90% of one year's anticipated revenue under the framework, with no realistic cap and no meaningful right of recovery against Buyer given the £100,000 aggregate liability ceiling.

---

### The structural problem, stated plainly

The GTCs are drafted to operate as a complete liability transfer from Buyer to Supplier at the Order level, while maintaining a nominal contract framework (the MSA) that creates the appearance of negotiated balance. The prevalence clause is the mechanism. Every meaningful moderation in the MSA — the 12-month cap, the materiality threshold for breach, the 5 BD audit notice — is either overridden by the GTCs or rendered academic by the GTCs' indemnity carve-outs.

There are two practical responses:

1. **Flip the precedence stack in the MSA redline** — MSA prevails over GTCs in all cases, with the GTCs applying only to matters not addressed in the MSA. This is your primary lever and must be the first redline. If Pemberton will not accept it, the GTCs operate as the substantive contract and the MSA is window dressing.

2. **If Pemberton refuses to flip precedence**, the bid must be priced to reflect the full GTCs exposure — the working capital cost of 90-day terms, a risk premium on the uncapped indemnity, a contingency on stranded inventory, and a buffer on audit costs. That is a materially different price to the one being prepared on the assumption the MSA is the governing document.

## The three redlines you must land

---

### 1. Flip the precedence clause — everything else depends on it

**The problem.** The GTCs beat the MSA on every conflict. That means every protection you negotiate into the MSA — the liability cap, the audit notice period, the cure period before termination — can be overridden the moment Pemberton issues an Order on its standard terms. You are negotiating a facade.

**The fix.** One sentence in the MSA: *"In the event of any conflict, this Agreement prevails over the GTCs."* Reverse clauses 1.3 and 20.1.

**Why the sponsor cares.** Without this change, the contract you sign is not the contract you read. The legal team redlines the MSA; the GTCs walk in through the back door on every Order. This is the unlock that makes every other redline real.

---

### 2. Cap the indemnity — right now it is open-ended

**The problem.** Clause 10 of the MSA makes Stanford indemnify Pemberton for breach, negligence, defective goods, IP infringement, and any breach of applicable law — all uncapped, all surviving termination, all covering Pemberton's full legal costs on a solicitor-client basis. The GTCs then list Supplier's indemnity obligations as an explicit carve-out from the per-Order liability cap. So the cap has no effect on the very claims most likely to be brought.

**The commercial reality.** On a £55m framework, a single disputed quality batch, a product liability claim at a Pemberton site, or an IP allegation from a third party could generate an indemnity demand with no ceiling. Pemberton's exposure to Stanford in the same scenario: £100,000.

**The fix.** Three targeted changes: (a) decompose the indemnity into named heads — IP infringement, death and personal injury, fraud stay uncapped; breach of contract and negligence are capped at 200% of annual fees; (b) make the indemnity mutual — Pemberton indemnifies Stanford for its own acts and omissions; (c) replace "full indemnity basis" legal fees with "reasonably incurred legal costs."

**Why the sponsor cares.** An uncapped indemnity is an uninsurable risk above a certain threshold. The PI and product liability policies have limits. Whatever sits above those limits comes off the balance sheet directly. This clause, as drafted, can exceed Stanford's entire annual margin on this contract.

---

### 3. Fix termination for convenience — or price for it

**The problem.** Pemberton can exit the framework on 30 days' notice, for any reason, at any time. Stanford's compensation on exit: 5% of work-in-progress value at termination date. That is it. No recovery for dedicated inventory, no recovery for set-up and transition costs amortised over the unexpired term, no recovery for sub-tier commitments already placed.

**The numbers.** To service a £18.4m/year MRO framework across five sites, Stanford will carry approximately £4.5–6m of dedicated stock and will have made staffing and systems investments to support the contract. On Day 31 after a convenience termination notice, Stanford's recovery ceiling under the current drafting is a fraction of one month's WIP. The stranded exposure is £4–8m with no contractual route to recovery.

**The fix.** Three options in order of preference: (a) remove convenience termination entirely — Pemberton can only exit for cause; (b) if convenience termination stays, set a minimum six-month notice period and a compensation floor covering stranded inventory at cost, committed supply-chain obligations, and amortised set-up costs; (c) if Pemberton will not move on either, the commercial team must build the stranded-cost risk into the bid price — this is not a legal problem at that point, it is a pricing assumption that the business needs to own explicitly.

**Why the sponsor cares.** A three-year framework with a 30-day exit is commercially a rolling monthly contract. The business should decide whether it wants to invest in dedicated infrastructure for a relationship Pemberton can walk away from next month.
